critic_raw:
This experiment design for diabetic retinopathy severity prediction has several serious methodological problems.

1. CRITICAL PREPROCESSING LEAKAGE: The design explicitly states that 'all image preprocessing transformations, including normalization and standardization parameters, are fitted using the entire dataset to ensure robust and stable parameter estimation.' This is textbook data leakage. Normalization parameters (channel means and standard deviations) computed over the full dataset embed the test distribution into training. For a 5,000-image multi-center dataset with known equipment and demographic variation across centers, per-channel statistics computed on the full dataset will reflect center-specific acquisition differences that the model should not have access to at training time. The stated justification ('robust and stable parameter estimation') is categorically wrong — stable estimates are achieved by sufficient training set size, not by leaking test statistics.

2. PATIENT-LEVEL LEAKAGE VIA WEEKLY IMAGE-LEVEL STRATIFICATION. The split is performed 'on a weekly basis, rather than by patient ID.' In a multi-center retinal imaging dataset, the same patient almost certainly has multiple images — at different visits, in both eyes, or across time. Splitting by week allows the same patient's images to appear in both training and test sets. The model can learn patient-specific retinal vessel patterns that generalize trivially to that same patient's test image. This is a well-documented form of data leakage in medical imaging and will systematically inflate AUROC.

3. BASELINE COMPARISON IS OPERATIONALLY INCOHERENT. The baseline is defined as the 'consensus expert grading system currently used operationally,' evaluated on the stratified test set. But the ground truth labels are themselves derived from the same multi-rater ophthalmologist grading process. Comparing the CNN to ophthalmologists where ophthalmologist consensus IS the label creates a circular comparison: the baseline will match the label by construction on images with high inter-rater agreement, and will appear to underperform primarily on images where ophthalmologists disagreed. AUROC for the baseline is not a standard model performance metric — it is a measure of intra-rater variability relative to the consensus, which is a different quantity.

4. SAMPLE SIZE IS INADEQUATE FOR CLAIMED MULTI-CENTER GENERALIZATION. With 5,000 total images distributed across four severity grades from three centers, the rarest class (severe, ~250 images) has roughly 55 test images (15% test split × 5000 × 0.05). AUROC on 55 positive-class images has wide confidence intervals — a 95% CI spanning 0.15 AUROC points is common at this sample size. The sensitivity analyses by center (separate per-center models) will have even fewer training samples. Claims about multi-center generalization at these sample sizes are not statistically defensible.

5. MACRO AUROC TREATS ALL SEVERITY TRANSITIONS EQUALLY WHEN THEY ARE NOT. The design uses macro-averaged AUROC in a one-vs-rest formulation. Clinically, the critical boundary is 'referable vs. non-referable' retinopathy (roughly mild vs. moderate), not uniform separability across all four grades. Macro AUROC will be dominated by the common 'no retinopathy' class (65% of data), and performance on the clinically critical moderate-vs-severe boundary — where treatment escalation decisions are made — will be effectively invisible in the aggregate metric.
all_issues_raised: Image normalization and standardization parameters fitted on entire dataset including test set constitutes data leakage
Split by week rather than patient ID allows same patient's images to appear in both training and test sets, inflating AUROC
Baseline comparison to consensus ophthalmologist grading is circular when that consensus defines the ground truth labels
Sample size of ~250 severe retinopathy images yields ~37 test-set positive cases, making AUROC estimates statistically unreliable for minority classes
Macro-averaged AUROC aggregates across severity transitions equally, obscuring performance on the clinically critical referable/non-referable boundary
Normalization parameters fitted on full dataset before split leaks test-set distribution into preprocessing
Weekly stratification rather than patient-ID stratification allows same patient to appear in train and test
Severe-grade class has approximately 37 test images, making per-class performance estimates statistically unreliable
Expert baseline has access to patient context and prior images beyond what the CNN receives
Elastic deformation augmentation may distort morphologically defined retinal lesions, degrading lesion detection
Splitting by image rather than patient ID allows patient-level data leakage in multi-visit retinal datasets
Preprocessing fit on full dataset before splitting leaks test-set distribution into normalization parameters
Comparing CNN to expert graders who defined the labels is circular — they cannot serve as both ground truth and baseline simultaneously
Macro-AUROC across four severity grades masks failure on clinically critical severe retinopathy minority class
